learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a broad understanding of the field of politics, including the essentially contested nature of many fundamental concepts.
Demonstrate an understanding of norms and institutions and the role they play in political life
Demonstrate the capacity to identify and analyse competing arguments
Demonstrate the capacity to write clearly and effectively

assessments.task_description:
This assessment requires students to write six reflective summaries of their learning due in Weeks 2, 3, 5, 7, 8, and 12.

The first five reflections should draw on required readings and seminar discussions for the relevant week.

The final reflective piece, due in Week 12, should reflect on students’ key learning across the course as a whole. This does not need to summarise each and every aspect of their learning, instead you may like to focus on your most notable learning, which may be about understanding a concept, theory or applying an analytical approach for political analysis. 

As a guide, each learning reflection should be a maximum of 300 words, except the final reflection which should be between 400-450 words.

The first five reflections are worth 3 marks each (i.e. total 15 marks), and the final reflection is worth 5 marks. A very short writing for example, less than 150 words receives a 0 mark.

This task is not a summary of the readings. Rather, it is an opportunity for students to demonstrate their learning through reflective writing.

Written feedback will not be provided for every submission; however, each submission will be checked to ensure the task has been completed and that students are eligible to receive the mark.

Deadlines:

  • First learning reflection: 7 August 2026
  • Second learning reflection: 14 August 2026
  • Third learning reflection: 28 August 2026
  • Fourth learning reflection: 11 September 2026
  • Fifth learning reflection: 18 September 2026
  • Final learning reflection: 23 October 2026

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Analytical op-ed with a theoretical appendix
assessments.task_description:
This assessment requires you to write an analytical op-ed suitable for publication in a major public-facing outlet (such as The Conversation, Australian Outlook, Foreign Policy, or The Interpreter). It must address a contemporary political issue related to one of the following themes:

  • Power, authority and legitimacy
  • Democracy and its threats

You should choose a case or event in relation to the above topic in the past two years.

The assessment has two parts; both must be submitted as a single document.

The first part is an analytical op-ed (15 marks).

In this part, present a clear and persuasive argument aimed at public audiences. Use accessible, non-academic language, and develop a focused analysis and argument. Where you draw on evidence or scholarship, weave it naturally into the text. This is how good political journalists and policy analysts often write. Where citations are necessary, embed them as hyperlinks within the text. Your goal is to communicate a complex political idea in a way that is engaging, accurate, and analytically sharp.

The second part is a short theoretical appendix (10 marks).

In this part you must explain the academic foundation of your argument in the op-ed. Identify the key political concepts and theoretical perspectives used, and briefly explain how those concepts connect to or inform the position you took. Include appropriate citations and references. The appendix must provide the conceptual reasoning behind your argument in the op-ed, not a summary of commentary or literature review. While the analytical op-ed is written for a public audience, the appendix demonstrates your capacity to connect your argument to relevant scholarly debates.

The word count for both components combined is 1,500 words. You should allocate approximately 1,000–1,100 words to the first part and 400–500 words to the second part. Citations and references are excluded from the word count.

This assessment is designed to develop students’ ability to translate complex political theory and concepts into analytical writing for wider audiences, while maintaining conceptual rigour and analytical depth.

An example and marking criteria will be available on the course Blackboard site.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your case analysis via the Turnitin link in Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment requires you to develop a clear plan and research strategy for your final essay.

It has two parts; both must be submitted as a single document.

Part One: Essay plan (5 marks)

In this part, you will produce a short essay plan of approximately 400 words. The plan should briefly outline the background to the issue or problem your essay will address, clearly state your research question, identify the key concepts and theories you will use in the final essay, and present a provisional argument or thesis statement. Your argument may evolve as the essay develops, but you should still aim to express it as clearly as possible at this stage, since it will guide your research and writing.

Part Two: Source identification and evaluation (10 marks)

This part assesses your ability to find, evaluate, and select sources for your essay. You will search for material using two different methods:

  • UQ Library search
  • A generative AI platform (e.g., Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude, and so on)

From each method, produce a list of seven sources, for a total of fourteen. Any source suggested by a generative AI platform must be independently verified. To do so check that it exists, that the citation details are accurate, and that it examines what the AI claims it does.

After reviewing all fourteen sources, select the eight most relevant and useful for your essay. Write a justification of up to 1,000 words explaining why you chose these eight, how you assessed their quality and credibility, and why they are a strong fit for your essay. This justification is not a review or summary of the sources themselves; it should demonstrate your ability to evaluate their relevance and suitability for your research. You may add further sources in the final essay as necessary.

References are excluded from the word count.

A list of essay questions will be available on the Blackboard site early in the semester. Students may also devise their own essay questions, provided they relate to one of the course topics.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your essay plan via the Turnitin link in Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment requires students to write a 3000-word essay based on their previously submitted essay plan.

You should expand on the aims, argument, and methods outlined in your plan. The essay requires you to engage in further reading and research, drawing on both course materials and relevant academic literature on your chosen topic.

When writing your essay, please ensure the following:

  • Present a clear and well-structured argument that directly answers the essay question.
  • Use appropriate scholarly sources and critically evaluate them. Build your own argument in response to the essay question, supported by this literature.
  • Clearly explain your reasoning, showing how your conclusion follows from the arguments you present.
  • Include relevant examples to support your analysis.
  • Write clearly and concisely, using correct grammar and academic expression.
  • Cite all sources accurately, using the recommended referencing style (APA 7th edition).
  • Proofread your essay carefully before submission, and check your Turnitin similarity score.

References are not included in the word count.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
